# `identity register` auto-binds both the session's identity NAME (existing
# behavior) and its TOKEN (new) — so a Tier-2 call in the same session can omit
# both --as and --token entirely afterward. Explicit --token/--token-file always
# overrides the bound one, and a bound token is never used to authenticate as a
# DIFFERENT identity than the one the session is actually bound to.
env BREEZE_DIR=$WORK/state
env BREEZE_SESSION_ID=test-session-1

# Registering binds this session to "admin" + admin's own token.
exec breeze identity register admin
cp stdout admin.token

# Register carol under a DIFFERENT simulated session so test-session-1 stays
# bound to admin (registering always rebinds the CURRENT session to whatever
# name was just registered).
env BREEZE_SESSION_ID=other-session
exec breeze identity register carol
env BREEZE_SESSION_ID=test-session-1

# No --as, no --token at all — both must be inferred from the session's bound
# admin identity/token for this real Tier-2 op to succeed.
exec breeze role assign builder carol
exec breeze role list --json
stdout '"name":\s*"carol"[\s\S]*"builder"'

# A wrong EXPLICIT token still overrides (and fails) rather than silently
# falling back to the bound one.
! exec breeze role assign reviewer carol --token-file wrong.token
stderr 'authentication failed'

# Switching --as to an identity the session ISN'T bound to must NOT silently
# reuse admin's bound token to authenticate as carol.
! exec breeze role assign reviewer carol --as carol
stderr 'this operation requires --as and --token'

exec breeze stop

-- wrong.token --
not-a-real-token
